title: ANH00026 Aortic Valve Replacement
text_en: The surgeon then separates the sternum to expose the pericardium, which is a protective membrane encircling the heart.

title: ANH00026 Aortic Valve Replacement
text_en: Your surgeon will then open the pericardium to expose the heart.

title: ANH00026 Aortic Valve Replacement
text_en: At this point, your heart will be connected to a heart lung machine. Tubes are used to rerot your blood into this machine, which takes over the functions of the heart and lungs during the operation.

title: ANH00026 Aortic Valve Replacement
text_en: Once the heart lung machine has taken over, your heart will be temporarily stopped and the blood inside removed. This keeps the aortic valve still and allows your surgeon to see it.

title: ANH00026 Aortic Valve Replacement
text_en: Your surgeon will open the aorta to expose the aortic valve, make an incision around the edge of the damaged valve, and remove it.

title: ANH00026 Aortic Valve Replacement
text_en: Your surgeon will then carefully sew the replacement valve into place and close the aorta with stitches.

title: ANH00026 Aortic Valve Replacement
text_en: At this point, blood will again be allowed to flow into your heart to check the function of the new valve. If needed, an electric shock will be given to restart the heart.

title: ANH00026 Aortic Valve Replacement
text_en: Once your heart is beating on its own, it will be detached from the heart lung machine.

title: ANH00026 Aortic Valve Replacement
text_en: Generally, temporary pacemaker wires will be placed on the surface of the heart to ensure the heart maintains a normal rhythm during recovery.

title: ANH00026 Aortic Valve Replacement
text_en: After surgery, you'll be taken to the intensive care unit where your vital functions will be monitored. You will still have a breathing tube in your throat, which will prevent you from talking. Several tubes will be in place to drain blood and fluid from your incision, and your bladder catheter will still be draining your urine.

title: ANH00028 Carotid Endarterectomy
text_en: Your doctor may recommend you get a carotid and dardirectomy if your carotid arteries are narrowed or blocked with plaque.

title: ANH00028 Carotid Endarterectomy
text_en: In your neck are two major blood vessels called the carotid arteries, which carry oxygen rich blood from your heart to your brain. You can feel the pulse of the carotid artery in the side of your neck.

title: ANH00028 Carotid Endarterectomy
text_en: Three deposits called atherosclerotic plaques can build up inside the carotid artery. These plaques can narrow the passageway of the artery and interfere with blood flow to the brain. This blockage or narrowing is called a stenosis.

title: ANH00028 Carotid Endarterectomy
text_en: If blood flow to the brain is blocked temporarily, a transient ischemic attack, also known as a TIA, may occur. When the blood flow is completely obstructed, as in a carotid artery blockage, a stroke

title: ANH00028 Carotid Endarterectomy
text_en: Before your procedure, an intravenous line will be started.

title: ANH00028 Carotid Endarterectomy
text_en: A carotid and dardirectomy is usually done under general anesthesia, which will put you to sleep for the duration of the procedure. In this case, a breathing tube will be inserted through your mouth and into your windpipe to help you breathe during the operation.

title: ANH00028 Carotid Endarterectomy
text_en: Sometimes a carotid and dardirectomy is done with local anesthesia. If local anesthesia is used, you will remain awake, but your neck will be numbed. You will probably also receive some sedation.

title: ANH00028 Carotid Endarterectomy
text_en: On the side of your neck along the blocked artery, the surgeon will make an incision that may run from just behind the ear to a point above the collarbone.

title: ANH00028 Carotid Endarterectomy
text_en: Your surgeon will find the carotid artery and temporarily clamp off blood flow through

title: ANH00028 Carotid Endarterectomy
text_en: Your surgeon will open up the artery and may place a shunt or temporary bypass to keep blood flowing to the brain.

title: ANH00028 Carotid Endarterectomy
text_en: Your surgeon will then remove the plaque within the arterial walls.

title: ANH00028 Carotid Endarterectomy
text_en: After the blockage has been removed, here she will sew the artery back together, usually placing a patch to widen the artery slightly. The patch may be made of part of one of your veins or an artificial material such as

title: ANH00028 Carotid Endarterectomy
text_en: Finally, your surgeon will close the incision with sutures or staples and cover it with A temporary drain is frequently left in the wound.

title: ANH00028 Carotid Endarterectomy
text_en: After your operation, you will be taken to a recovery area where the staff will monitor your vital signs and neurologic condition. You will be given pain medication as needed and will gradually progress from a liquid to a solid diet.

file: ANH00029_Prevention of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism.mp4
title: ANH00029 Prevention of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism
text_en: If you are a mobile for an extended period of time because of surgery, pregnancy, illness, or travel, you may be at risk for deep vein thrombosis or DVT. A condition where a blood clot forms in a deep vein in the body, typically the leg.

file: ANH00029_Prevention of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism.mp4
title: ANH00029 Prevention of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism
text_en: Unlike arteries which rely on the heart to force blood through them, veins transport blood with the help of muscle contractions while you move and walk. Plans also have valves that keep blood from flowing backward and pooling in your arms and legs.

file: ANH00029_Prevention of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism.mp4
title: ANH00029 Prevention of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism
text_en: When you are a mobile for an extended period, your venous circulation slows down. As a result, clotting factors in the blood may cause the slow moving blood to clump together, forming a blood clot or thrombus. This condition is called deep vein thrombosis or DVT.

file: ANH00029_Prevention of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism.mp4
title: ANH00029 Prevention of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism
text_en: Common symptoms of DVT include swelling of the leg or a vein in the leg, pain or tenderness in the area, increased warmth in the skin, and red or discolored skin.

file: ANH00029_Prevention of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism.mp4
title: ANH00029 Prevention of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism
text_en: In some DVT cases, a blood clot may detach from a deep vein and join the bloodstream. The clot travels from the deep veins of the leg through the veins of the abdomen, into the heart, and finally lodges in the pulmonary artery or its branches, blocking blood flow to the lungs.

file: ANH00029_Prevention of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism.mp4
title: ANH00029 Prevention of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism
text_en: This condition called pulmonary embolism is very serious and may permanently damage a portion of the lung. If a clot is large or if there are many clots, pulmonary embolism may also cause death.
